title: Practical gates by Majorana fermion motion
authors: Yuri D. Lensky, Bryce Kobrin, Kostyantyn Kechedzhi, Igor Aleiner
abstract: Quantum error correction protocols protect against local errors by storing logical information non-locally. This poses a challenge: how to design efficient logical gates on the non-local ``hidden'' logical information, and how to implement these gates using the local physical operations. We develop a general description of planar Pauli stabilizer codes and protocols for logical operations in terms of point-like particles called Majorana fermions. Information is stored in the pairwise fermion parities of spatially separated Majorana fermions. The description in terms of Majorana fermions captures not only large distance asymptotics, but also all scales down to the lattice constants. We exploit this locality to densely pack logical information in spacetime. The simplest application is to a static case: dense memory. More importantly, we implement fault-tolerant Majorana motion and leverage this primitive to design braiding-based logical gates. This approach reduces space overhead of logical operations resulting in an improved logical error rate given fixed number of physical qubits. We illustrate a practical use of our approach by designing and benchmarking of 2-qubit Clifford gates. We find numerically that our protocol outperforms lattice surgery in this setting for near-term error rates and realistic device constraints. More generally, introduction of compact motion of Majorana fermions as an efficient computational primitive opens a promising new route for the design of low overhead error correction protocols.
summary: This paper proposes using the controlled motion and braiding of Majorana fermions as a fundamental, low-overhead primitive for performing logical quantum gates. By mapping Pauli stabilizer codes onto these fermion dynamics, the authors show that this braiding approach significantly outperforms established methods like lattice surgery in terms of logical error rate scaling. This offers a promising new architectural route for building scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computers.
main_problem: The core challenge is designing and implementing efficient, fault-tolerant logical gates on non-local quantum information stored within quantum error correction (QEC) codes.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that using Majorana fermion motion and braiding provides a superior, lower-overhead protocol for implementing 2-qubit Clifford gates compared to standard lattice surgery.
method: The work develops a general description of planar Pauli stabilizer codes using Majorana fermions, mapping logical operations to physical particle motion (braiding).
model_or_system: The system models quantum information stored in planar Pauli stabilizer codes realized by the parities of spatially separated Majorana fermions on a lattice.
key_observables: Logical error rate (LER), gate fidelity, code distance ($\ell_c$), and the comparison metric $\eta_{ eta 	o \alpha}$ between protocols.
important_parameters: Code distance ($d$), physical qubit count, and the ratio of logical error rates between braiding and lattice surgery protocols.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the feasibility of implementing the protocol on hardware supporting the surface code and uses specific error models for comparison.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate memory encoding using Majorana fermions (Wilson/t'Hooft lines), the mapping of Pauli operators to four Majorana fermions, and direct comparisons of gate fidelity vs. qubit number showing the advantage of braiding over lattice surgery.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the Majorana fermion description for Pauli codes, then detailing how Majorana motion generates logical gates, and finally benchmarking this braiding approach against lattice surgery to prove its superior scaling and fidelity.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, physically motivated framework for realizing fault-tolerant quantum computation using topological excitations (Majorana fermions), which is highly relevant for superconducting or topological quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Several memories written in terms of Majorana fermions. The vertical dimension in all the figures is d (in the metric described in the main text), and is 2d (d) for Figs. 1b and 1c (Fig. 1a). The wavy lines are Wilson lines (see main text). A choice for which Pauli operator they correspond in the logical encoding is shown in Figs. 1a and 1b. All blue and yellow Wilson lines have the same length.

FIG. 3. Outline of the steps of the Majorana embedding, described in more detail in [2]. First, a set of arrowed tetrahedra for n qubits is actually a diagrammatic representation of a specific embedding of their Hilbert space into that of 4n Majorana fermions. Majorana bilinears associated to a single qubit, shown with colors here and elsewhere, are images of Pauli operators under this embedding. This requires a choice of ordering on the bilinears, represented as arrows in the picture. The choice of such arrows gives rise to a background gauge field we call Z(K) 2 . The systematic procedure for choosing a gauge and physical consequences of this gauge field are discussed in [2], and for...

FIG. 4. An example code where code distance is determined by the ’t Hooft line shown in green, that is shorter than the Wilson line shown in blue. Pauli Z label errors that correspond to the ’t Hooft line. Following the rules of Sec. II we determine that ℓH = 3 whereas ℓW = 5. We also show an example Wilson line in red, which has one end on an undimerized Majorana fermion and another on a dimerized one. The procedure for writing Wilson lines as Pauli strings is detailed in [2].
FIG. 5. Majorana graph for a small instance of dense mem- ory. Blue and yellow lines show Wilson line undetectable errors, see Fig. 1(c).

FIG. 7. Virtual particle motion for Majorana fermions. We move the Majorana fermion at the top middle to the location indicated by the dashed line. The blue lines indicate the Majorana fermion pairs created from vacuum, 1. They are fused in the pairs indicated by the red lines. These fusion outcomes are random; on the right, we show a scenario where two of the fusions result in ε.
